turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: We'll hear argument now in Number oh oh eight six oh, Colre- Correctional Services Corporation versus John Malesko. Mr. Phillips.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court. The issue in this case is whether an action for damages under Bivens should be applied to a private corporation acting under color of Federal law. Like many cases, where you come out on a case like this, I think in many ways depends on where you begin, and the parties have put forward to this Court fundamentally conflicting paradigms with respect to the best way to analyze Bivens based on this Court's prior decisions. The respondent and the court below essentially concluded that Bivens is a ubiquitous remedy that ought, generally, to be available in order to maximize recoveries and to maximize, or at least optimize, deterrent values, and that it is our burden essentially to try to ascertain whether there might be some conflicting or some exception to the Bivens doctrine that would get us out from under liability in the in this particular case. [Inaudible]

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: The... Mr. Phillips,

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: do you at, this this involves only an action against the corporation not against its employees.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: That's correct, Justice O'Connor.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: Would if the action were brought as a Bivens action against the employees, do you concede that there would be a Bivens action against them?

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: W- We have always assumed, from the first day of this li- litigation, that a Bivens action would lie against...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: the individual employees. And...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: there's a difference between assuming it arguendo and conceding it.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Well, for purposes of this litigation and for purposes of my client, there's no question we would concede that the, an action would have legitimately been br- been raised against them.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Bivens should be extended is a separate question.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: concession holds, if the employee is sued for a wrongful act, under State law would it be permissible in your view, just under s- standard principles of derivative liability, to hold the corporation for that tort, for the tort of its own employee?

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: You mean under a theory of respondeat...

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: superior.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: As a matter of State law, it's going to depend on the State.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: superior liability.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: State courts could do that without interference with any Federal policy or with or without any s- super, superseding F- Federal law to the contrary.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Well, o- obviously, there's going to be at least a potential argument raised with respect to Boyle and whether or not the the decision to hold the individual liable under those circumstances is preempted under Boyle.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: to...

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: I assume, would routinely be named in in the suit.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Well, I think you're doing an extraordinary amount, Justice Kennedy.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: circumstances.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Mr. Mr. Phillips, if the if the proper way of looking at this is the principal agency relationship, when you're dealing with the Federal Government, the Federal Government is the principal, the agent is the officer.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: The the premise of your question, Justice Ginsburg, I think is where the where the mistake lies in the final outcome of the decision.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: defendants under those circumstances.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: here were arguing on behalf of the employee.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: No, Justice...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: to that effect.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Well, it the question ultimately comes down to this, Y- Y- Your Honor, is that does it make any more sense in this context to resolve this issue at this point in time than it did to decide the FDIC versus Meyer case at that point in time.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Federal right.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Strictly speaking, Mr. Phillips, for you to say that you're making a concession that the employee I mean, that's like a lawyer representing A saying he concedes B would be liable.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: I mean, it's not much of a concession.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: simply answering Justice Kennedy and Justice O'Connor's question.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: concession, to be sure.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: I'm not giving up much i- in that regard.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Mr. Phillips...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: on the basis that they're an agent of an agent, that we we're not going to track it that far down.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: draw the line.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: be an argument, but in order to do that, Justice Scalia, you would then have to abandon what was one of the principal legs of Bivens in the first instance, which is that the litigation against the private individual and the deterrent value of litigation against the private individual is the most significant way to achieve the overall objectives...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Well, I don't know that we would...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: have to abandon that because the the concern, a- as I understand it, that the action against the individual has a more significant deterrent effect than the action against the agency, was a concern that was expressed in the context of dealing with a public agency.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: I I think the the flaw in your analysis of the deterrence theory, Justice Souter, is that you're looking to figure out what is sort of the optimal answer for deterrence.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: When you go beyond I'm sorry.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: fi- finish.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: But when you go beyond that, it seems to me you then assume much more of a legislative role.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: as long as we have an adequate...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: that argument far enough, then there would be no liability at all because we know that if we hold, for example, the individual liable, there is going to be a tendency there to shift that liability either by insurance or by respondeat superior and insurance, ultimately to the cost of contracting.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Well ob- ob- we don't obviously, we don't ask the Court to overrule Bivens.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: problem...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: adequate remedy? The the as far as deterrence is concerned, I thought Richardson explains why the deterrence considerations with the private company work perfectly, but they don't work at all where the principal is the Federal agency.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: The the...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: far as alternative remedy is concerned, which alternative remedy? If you mean would there be a remedy under State law, that's of course exists in Bivens too.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: case the individual, if we say you can sue the company, would say the same thing.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Well, you made two you made two points, Justice Breyer.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: if you- if you're going to allow corporations, they say, oh, no, you have a private remedy against the individual, which I'm sure you conceded for that reason.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: I concede that you have a perfectly valid remedy, yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: What? That's right. Exactly. That's right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Indeed, I I I may be making the argument...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: given that conundrum,

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: deterrence, Richardson.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Well.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Adequate remedy, the problem we stated.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Well, now now you've made...

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: three points, Justice Breyer.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Well.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: where, heretofore, we have never impo-

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: this Court has never imposed. I'm sorry.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: in nineteen eighty-three, too, in Monell we rejected the idea there could be any sort of respondeat superior liability.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: That's absolutely...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: correct, Mr. Chief Justice.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Mr. Phillips? Because the policy of saying people who live above below the fifth floor, that that's a policy set by the employer.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Well, there are two answers to that, Justice Ginsburg.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: who was named as a defendant...

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: not in the written policy.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Well, I I you can construe it most favorably to the plaintiff, but not necessarily to em- embrace a complete different theory of the case that does, is far from clear.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Very well, Mr. Phillips.

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: for...

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: as arguendo, or are you conceding that? Now, your brief seems to make a concession to that effect.

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: In the first instance, we think it should be assumed arguendo because if there's a reason not to subject the individuals, the private individuals, to liability under Bivens, it would be that in private individuals have so few immunities and so few defenses, compared to their governmental counterparts, that there's no reason to infer a Federal cause of action.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Why couldn't you...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: say the same about joint tortfeasors?

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: Pardon? Oh.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Why couldn't you make the same kind of argument about joint tortfeasors? You'd say there's no reason to hold two.

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: Well, in fact, with respect to joint tortfeasors, you have two separate actions.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: for their...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: the the second when you'd say we'd only give you one, whoever you sue first, because it's adequate.

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: Well, you need to deter both of the joint act- the actions by the joint tortfeasors.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: And here we have to deter...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: the policies of the corporation.

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: Yes, but the corporation is an unusual tortfeasor in this sense, in that it cannot act except through ind- other individuals,

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: through its employees. So long as you deter...

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: whole law of torts.

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: Well, if this were a common law court or a legislature, I could certainly see adopting the common law rule.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: So, the role of the Court is not to establish...

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: just doesn't ring true to me that there's no deterrence by holding the corporation liable.

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: Well, in fact, Your Honor, if you look at, for example, the fifth edition of Prosser and Keeton on Torts, William Prosser tells us that that argument is makeweight, and that the real reason for holding the corporation liable under respondeat superior is to ensure that the costs of accidents are incorporated into the price of products and, therefore, spread to society at large.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: money...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: purchaser gets its money from everybody.

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: the court...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: that's a different argument which which you make, that we shouldn't...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: May I ask you...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: It would be, in effect, the same as holding the Government liable.

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: Well, in this case, where where you have a uniquely governmental purchaser and a uniquely governmental cause of action, it does tend to have that effect,

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Your Honor.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: hypothetical that Mr. Phillips' last argument suggested to me? Supposing you have a case in which an executive sets the policy that everybody has to climb the stair- the six flights of stairs every day.

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, when pri- when prisoners in public institutions, of federally operated institutions, encounter precisely that...

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: situation, it -- Right.

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: But th- it shows that this that type of situation is hardly unique to a private corporation.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: All the employee is doing is carrying out his instructions.

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: Right, but but there is under Bivens no Nuremberg defense.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Supposing...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: policy also said any employee who fails to carry out this policy gets fired forthwith.

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: That would be the same thing if a cons- if an individual Bureau of...

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Prisons employee enforce the Constitutional...

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: Individually liable.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Why does it make a large difference whether you sue the CEO or the corporation itself? Practically in terms of your interest as the Government money, if the corporation is going to pick up the tab, why does the Government care?

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: Well, I, Justice Ginsburg, I think the rub is in the question, if the go- corporation is going to pick up the tab.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: pay constitutional rights...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: judgments against the corporation than than than against Jack Armstrong personally.

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: That is one of the difficulties and that is one of the reasons why w- there is concern that individuals, if given the opportunity, will choose to sue only the corporation and not the individuals.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: violate the...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Cons-

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: If if the Government...

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: your position and impose Bivens liability on the corporation, I assume Congress can't do anything about that absent some supplemental scheme that's equally effective?

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: Your Honor, it's not clear the degree to which Congress can replace Bivens liability.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: I I don't understand that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: No, Your Honor. As I read Bivens...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: just just decides that we're wrong in saying that there's a Bivens remedy here, what what could what could Congress possibly do about it? I mean, if if there is a Bivens remedy here, it's one that's demanded by the Constitution.

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, it depends on how you read Bivens.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Where...

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: the Court op- or ac- where the Court uses its discretion to do that, however, the Court must be particularly cautious about it so that it does not usurp the role of Congress as the principal creator of c- of causes of action for damages under the Constitution.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Lamken.

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: [NS] Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: President.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: if Bivens remedies are available against the employees of the corporation, why isn't that enough?

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: Because of the mentality that exists as far as the corporation, Your Ho- Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: has the contract.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: can only act through its employees, and if those employees are subject to Bivens liability, if they are, why isn't that enough...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: to deter any...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: unconstitutional conduct?

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: It wouldn't be sufficient because the employees are at-will employees.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: While the cor-

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: generalize that the employees are at-will employees? I mean, that would vary perhaps from case to case.

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: In the State of New York, they're are at-will employees.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: we're deciding a case not just on the basis of the State of New York, but maybe in some other States, they have a three year contract.

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: It may vary from State to State.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Mr. Pasternak, do you think running municipally owned utility is a core governmental function?

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: It's possible that it may be.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: May be.

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: Getting further removed.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: prison situation.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: a whole new area of constitutional or quasi constitutional law deciding case by case what is a core governmental function and what isn't a core government you you you assert that only only those corporations that are performing core governmental functions would would be subject to Bivens liability, not all corporations who are under contract with the Government.

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: If I may respond.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Ha- Have i- is is there a whole line of cases under section nineteen eighty-three dealing with this subject of what is a core governmental function?

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: There's not a whole line of cases, but there is a, there are cases, Your Honor, that have to be addressed as far as either a Federal Tort Claims Act case or a section nineteen eighty-three as to the issue of whether or not Amtrak, for instance whether it would be acting as a Government agent...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: or not.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Does it have to do with a, whether it's performing a core governmental function or not? It has to do with whether it's a Federal agency or not.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Those lines of thinking...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: concededly private corporation, and and you you want us to decide case by case when w- w- when you hire a private corporation to manage concessions at a at a national park, whether that is a core governmental function or not.

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: I think any time you're dealing with a corporation as apposed [: opposed] to a Government agency, there are different things that come into play as far as what their motives are.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: I thought you just wanted us to decide a case involving prisons, which can be run by a State, by the Federal Government, or can be contracted out by either.

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: That is what our fact...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: pattern is.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: a substitute for a Federal prison just as sometimes Federal prisoners are housed in State prisons.

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: And would there b- be liability in that situation? Suppose this halfway house had been run by the State of New York.

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: Then there wouldn't be liability for the violation of the Federal Constitution, and there would be no difference.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: And as to the...

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: so, if if Mr. Malesko had been a State prisoner?

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: Then i- u- he would have his claim.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: May I clarify a point that I raised to Mr. Phillips? Is your complaint one of a pattern or practice attributable to the corporation as distinguished from the action of the individual guard? He said that you did not plead any kind of policy on behalf of the corporation.

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: There are different elements as far as what was pleading i- in the complaint.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Was that pleaded a- as a policy?

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: Not pleaded directly as a policy, no.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: You say not pleaded directly as a policy.

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: Well, i- again, we're going back to the language of the pro se complaint.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: He did not allege it as a policy per se.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: on...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: he did allege that he was permitted to to use the elevator usually, didn't he?

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: He did.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: Well, there's still a complaint that would exist wi- I mean, there is a possible claim as far as the ADA and just the general logic of housing somebody with a known heart condition on the fifth floor where he would be susceptible to being killed in the event of a fire.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: I thought his heart attack made it hard to climb stairs, but going down stairs might be different.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: I would I would like to hear directly your your response.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: In here...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: should there be respondeat superior? Is it necessary to parallel the Monell? What's your opinion?

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: I would argue that under either situation, we would satisfy the requirement.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: If we're looking for parallelism between the two, then it would make sense to have the Monell standard, but it not necessarily has to follow because under common law, we would have the respondeat superior.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Well, we rejected...

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: parallelism in Richardson.

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: It was rejected and symmetry is difficult to achieve.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Well, you're arguing...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: the so-called parallelism with section nineteen eighty-three actions.

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: It has been limited.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Why shouldn't we leave this to the Federal Government, to the Congress, to determine, rather than doing it ourselves?

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: For the same...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: this is a a totally different situation from what Bivens, whether it was right or wrong, decided.

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: Oftentimes.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: It may be logical to extend it.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: And also, there there is undoubtedly a State law cause of action in negligence that would lie against both the employee and the employer under respondeat superior.

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: There would be a common law cause of action.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: But would there have been a State cause of action against Federal agents acting under under Federal law?

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: You would have the issue as far as normally you would have the immunity that would be granted under the State law, and you would have the problems in that direction.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: W- Which means that...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: there's a special reason for the court to invent a cause of action that does not appear anywhere in the Constitution or in a statute for Federal agents who perhaps can't be sued under State law.

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: Since we are dealing with a violation of the Federal Constitution, we would submit that there should be one uniform body of decision coming out as far as what that Federal law should be and how it should be interpreted, that we should not be subjected to the vagaries of the differences in the various States as to their rules as far as procedure, discovery, but there should be one uni- you- yiel- one body of law encompassing, from the Federal side, a violation of a Federal constitutional right.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Well, i- is it clear that the us- employee would be liable under State law in light of Boyle versus United Technologies?

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: [NS] In McKnight, the argument w- with, rej- rejected the argument of Boyle, as far as a corporation being lia- n- not being liable and a danger as far as the public fisc.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Well, of course, since McKnight rejected parallelism, and and and you you want it.

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: Not entirely, but we are seeking to have the parallelism also as far as a F- rights of a Federal prisoner and a State prisoner, to both be able to go and sue the corporation...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: that is res-

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: have an anomaly either way because why shouldn't the symmetry be between someone who is a Federal prisoner in a Federal prison and someone who's a Federal prisoner in a private prison? You recognize that there would be no action against the Bureau of Prisons if the Bureau of Prisons had been the jailer.

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: Because the Correctional Services Corporation is not the Government.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: But you want it to be like the State government, because if it were a State if a State were running this prison, then there would be liability.

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: Yes, but what I'm looking for is it for not for it to be recognized by this Court that the private corporation, just like the State, is not the Federal Government.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: With respect to the adequacy of a remedy in the State court, is it not so that the liability would be greater as to a private employee because the private employee would not have a defense of qualified immunity that would be available to a Federal officer?

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: There would be less defenses raised as far as a private individual.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: And that would tend to make the State law remedy more adequate?

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: Possibly more adequate but still inadequate in order to address a Federal constitutional violation.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: Well, you're saying that this, I think you're saying the State law remedy simply doesn't address the constitutional violation.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: You know, there's one puzzling thing about this case.

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: It does it is an Eighth Amendment, but it is not cited.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: S-

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: therefore, your burden in the Federal claim under the Eighth Amendment is heavier than in an ordinary negligence case, isn't it? So, by going into Federal court, you you've assumed a higher burden than if you brought a negligence case.

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: Well, both theories would be applicable as far as being the, bringing the suit.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: a standard which we submit we would that my client, in filing the suit pro se, s- satisfied.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Was this complaint...

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: ever amended since your client gained representation, or is we still operating under the pro se complaint?

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: It was amended only for the purposes of na- of attempting to name Mr. Urena as a defendant, and then there was a proposed second amended complaint which would be naming additional defendants, which and I believe al- also different causes of actions, but that was denied...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: at that time.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: At at it's puzzling why there wasn't enough time to identify that individual, Mr. Urena.

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: My appearance came after the time period.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Mr. Lamken said that's not necessarily so, that the Government would pay.

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: Not necessarily so, but it is usually the case that happens.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: All insurance companies are going to increase rates if the corporation if they know that the corporations are going to be liable.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Well, right now, respectfully...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: they're they're not dumb and and they're competitive.

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: But no one is ar- respectfully, no one is arguing that there could not be a theory for CSC to be liable under common law, respondeat superior, or under the ADA or any other theory for the corporation to be liable.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: for it.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: I, You can't have it both ways.

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: If I may, I don't think that it necessarily comes out of the Government, because, A, you have the insurance, and B, if the rates get increased to the company, then the company then has to turn around and bid it out to the Federal Government, and the Federal Government can turn around and go to a different contractor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: employees...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: contractor that it would pass it on to w- would not be subject to the same law that that the, that we hold today?

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: He would be subject to the...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: same law.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: his rates would be higher too, wouldn't it?

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: Well, we would also a- anytime an insurance company is setting rates, they're going to be looking to its past history of the individual applicant to ascertain what the potential is and what programs are in place in order to try and control what those potential dangers are.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Mr. Pasternak, compare for a moment, if you will, the elements of damages you would be seeking under the kind of claim you have in mind and the elements of damages you might be seeking under a State a State law negligence action w- as as to amounts, amounts of money.

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: [NS] As far as amounts, it's difficult to quantify as to what it is.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Yes, but i- the w- what you want as a result of the claimed violation is money damages, I take it.

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: And I'm I'm asking you would the money damages be any different under, i- you know, under a theory of negligence as opposed to the theory you're proposing.

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: I think it would depend on how a jury viewed...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: the elements...

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: I mean I I realize one jury can give you one result and one another.

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: That would be.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Except you might have punitive damages under State law, might not you, if if indeed it was it was gross negligence or in- indeed virtually an intentional harming of of the prisoner? Under many State laws, you'd be able to get punitive damages, wouldn't you?

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: There would be a potential under certain situations under State law...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: to obtain punitive damages.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: as far as I know, we haven't held that punitive damages are available under Bivens, have we?

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: A a a State cause of action could a could a State just incorporate the Eighth Amendment standard as a matter of State tort law?

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: I'm sorry.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Could the State, as part of a State cause of action, simply e- incorporate the Eighth Amendment as part of a State tort standard?

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: There could...

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: no reckless indifference or you're you're liable under State law.

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: It could.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: decision making.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: don't need the Bivens cause of action even even for the Eighth Amendment because States are free to enact it if they choose.

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: But that would leave to each individual State to ascertain and decide whether or not there should be a remedy that exists as far as a Federal constitutional violation, which is a separate and distinct harm that needs to be addressed.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Is the same thing true about other Bivens actions?

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: This Court ha- yes, it is.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Well well the, you have no authority to say that a State can create a cause of action against a Federal officer.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Not as to a Federal officer, correct.

turns.name: STEVEN PASTERNAK
turns.text: In here, though, where we have a private corporation, who is operating pursuant to its contract with the Government, where it has a separate motive, a fiduciary duty in order to maximize its profits, and there the danger to the Federal fisc is less, we submit, or certainly no greater than to do additional Bivens situation, we would submit that the greatest deterrence that could exist is to go and permit the suit against the corporation and not impose the duty in order to try and ascertain which employee would it be, whether or not it's a former employee who set a policy who's no longer there, but to permit it because it is the corporation who has control of its individual employees based on the relationship, whether it be for a limited contract and what contract terms are or whether it be an at-will employee, and also based on respondeat superior, that we need to recognize that we are dealing with a private corporation.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Pasternak.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Mr.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: -- Go on ahead.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: quick question? Do you concede, for purposes of the, of our review of the case and based on your question presented, that the complaint states a cause of action?

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: For purposes of where the case is right at...

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: the moment, yes. I...

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: think on remand, obviously we would fight if if the case were to go forward, we would continue to fight that issue.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Mr. Phillips, i- is it, is it correct and and some of the questions, including my own, have assumed that you can't sue a Federal officer in State, you can't sue a Federal officer for a tort committed in the course of his official duties.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: It's immediately removable.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: there's no Federal prohibition against the suit, is there?

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Well, there will be a preemption issue that's going to immediately arise as to...

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: whether or not he's immune...

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: whether he's immune.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: an immunity...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: but but you if if it's not, you you you can sue him and and the only prohibition is, if he wants, he he can remove it to Federal court.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: CARTER G. PHILLIPS
turns.text: Thank you, Your Honor.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Phillips.
